one_line_definition: Randomly assigning test samples or test order to prevent systematic confounding of variables
Concealing test parameters or expected outcomes from the experimenter to prevent unconscious bias
Tests on identical samples with only the variable of interest changed, providing a baseline for comparison
Running the same experiment multiple times to estimate within-group variation and detect random effects
The probability of detecting a real effect of a given size if one exists; formally 1 − β
The probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as observed under the null hypothesis
Difference between group means divided by the control-group mean; a proxy for effect size when SD is unavailable
Standardised measure of practical significance: difference between means divided by the population standard deviation
Standard deviation expressed as a percentage of the mean; a measure of relative variability
Tendency to publish statistically significant or positive results and suppress null or negative findings
Claiming an effect exists when it does not; probability is controlled by the significance threshold α
Widespread failure to reproduce published scientific findings across psychology, medicine, and other fields
Registering study design and analysis plan before data collection to reduce outcome-reporting bias
Intersection over union of two binary-rated sets; used here to measure inter-rater agreement on study criteria
Systematic survey and critical evaluation of existing research relevant to a study's topic
The area of unanswered questions or unresolved problems that a new study aims to address
Integrating findings from multiple sources to form a new, coherent perspective rather than merely summarising
Linguistic devices (might, perhaps, possible) that reduce a writer's commitment to a claim
Linguistic devices (in fact, clearly, definitely) that assert the writer's certainty or confidence in a claim
Verbs used to attribute claims to prior authors (shows, showed, has shown); tense encodes epistemological stance
Restating source material in new words and sentence structure to avoid plagiarism while retaining meaning
The broader theoretical framework within which a study's focal theory is situated
The specific theory directly under investigation in a research study
Five-category rubric (Coverage, Synthesis, Methodology, Significance, Rhetoric) for evaluating PhD literature reviews
